title: Setting a World Record for MoE Pre-Training on NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA's GB300 NVL72 hit a world-record 1,648 TFLOPs/GPU pre-training DeepSeek-V3 671B (MoE), roughly 3x GB200 NVL72, driven by rack-scale NVLink co-design plus continuous software gains (Megatron Core, TorchTitan, JAX) that keep lifting the same silicon 1.5x-10x over months.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's GB300 NVL72 rack-scale system set a world record of 1,648 TFLOPs per GPU pre-training the DeepSeek-V3 671B mixture-of-experts model.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Sets a new bar for delivered (not just peak) training throughput on frontier-scale MoE models, a benchmark competitors and customers will be measured against.
insights.quote: NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 set a world record for pre-training DeepSeek-V3 671B at 1,648 TFLOPs per GPU

insights.claim: On the same 256-GPU DeepSeek-V3 671B pre-training workload, GB300 NVL72 with Megatron Core reaches 1,648 TFLOPs/GPU versus 606 TFLOPs/GPU on the prior-generation GB200 NVL72, roughly 3x higher delivered throughput per GPU in a single hardware generation.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: A 3x per-GPU gain in one generation implies customers can hit the same training performance with roughly a third of the racks, materially changing the economics of frontier pre-training.
insights.locator: Exceptional pre-training performance with Megatron Core

insights.claim: On the same GB300 NVL72 rack-scale hardware, pre-training performance on DeepSeek-V3 671B rose 1.5x over six months purely from software improvements, with no hardware changes.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Signals that NVIDIA's effective compute output keeps climbing after a chip ships, meaning today's benchmark comparisons against competitors likely understate NVIDIA's real trajectory.
insights.quote: This shows that raw performance and training throughput continue to improve well after the silicon ships.
insights.locator: 1.5x higher performance in six months from software alone

insights.claim: NVIDIA's contributions to TorchTitan (PyTorch's native training stack) compounded to deliver roughly 6x higher delivered performance on DeepSeek-V3 671B on the same GB300 NVL72 infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Shows NVIDIA extracting large software-only gains from an open-source framework it does not own, extending the value of hardware already sold.
insights.locator: GB300 NVL72 performance evolution with TorchTitan optimizations

insights.claim: Over six months, NVIDIA's JAX optimizations lifted DeepSeek-V3 671B pre-training performance on GB300 NVL72 by nearly 10x at 256-GPU scale, reaching 1,025 TFLOPS/GPU, entirely from software.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: A near-10x software-only gain on an already-shipped platform suggests JAX-based training workloads were previously leaving significant NVIDIA hardware performance on the table.
insights.locator: GB300 NVL72 performance evolution with JAX optimizations

insights.claim: NVIDIA states the current record-setting results are not a ceiling, framing continued co-designed hardware-software optimization as an ongoing performance trajectory rather than a one-time launch milestone.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Investors should expect further TFLOPs/GPU gains on already-installed GB300 fleets, effectively depreciating training costs for customers over time without new capex.
insights.quote: The record-setting performance of today is only the foundation for even higher performance tomorrow.
insights.locator: Setting a world record, closing paragraph

themes.theme: MoE economics and the communication bottleneck

insights.claim: Unlike dense models where every token activates every parameter, DeepSeek-V3 holds 671B total parameters but activates only about 37B parameters per token, reaching frontier-scale capability at the per-token compute cost of a much smaller model.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Explains why MoE has become the industry's default architecture for frontier models: it decouples parameter count (capability) from per-token compute cost (serving/training economics).
insights.locator: The rapid industry shift toward these MoE architectures

insights.claim: As MoE lowers compute per token, network communication (dispatching and gathering tokens across expert GPUs in every forward and backward pass) increasingly becomes the factor that limits how efficiently models scale across thousands of GPUs.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI infrastructure competition from a pure FLOPs race to a networking/interconnect race, favoring vendors (like NVIDIA) with tightly integrated scale-up fabrics over commodity GPU clusters.
insights.quote: As compute per token falls, communication increasingly determines how efficiently models scale across thousands of GPUs.

insights.claim: Because the MoE all-to-all communication step occurs at every layer of every training step, small per-step delays compound until communication can no longer be hidden behind compute, at which point adding more GPUs stops increasing throughput.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific failure mode that caps naive GPU-cluster scaling for MoE training, explaining why interconnect quality (not GPU count) becomes the binding constraint.

insights.claim: MoE pre-training presents a two-tiered communication challenge: a tightly coupled scale-up domain needing full bisection, non-blocking bandwidth within a rack, plus lighter but still latency-predictable scale-out traffic linking multiple racks together.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Gives a mental model for why single-metric GPU comparisons (e.g., peak FLOPs) are misleading for MoE workloads; both intra-rack and inter-rack network design must be evaluated.
insights.locator: This is why pre-training needs a tightly coupled scale-up domain

insights.claim: NVIDIA frames pre-training success as measured in delivered FLOPs at scale rather than peak (theoretical) FLOPs advertised on a spec sheet.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: An implicit critique of spec-sheet comparisons across GPU vendors; investors evaluating AI infrastructure should demand delivered-throughput benchmarks, not peak TFLOPs claims.
insights.quote: The challenge is two-tiered, and success is measured in delivered FLOPs, not peak FLOPs.
insights.locator: This is why pre-training needs a tightly coupled scale-up domain

insights.claim: Fifth-generation NVLink gives each of the 72 Blackwell Ultra GPUs in a GB300 NVL72 rack 1.8 TB/s of bandwidth and 130 TB/s of non-blocking all-to-all bandwidth across the rack, so every GPU reaches every other GPU in a single hop.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: This scale-up bandwidth is the concrete enabler behind the 3x TFLOPs/GPU jump over GB200; it is a hard-to-replicate systems advantage versus disaggregated GPU cluster designs.
insights.locator: At its core is NVIDIA NVLink

insights.claim: NVLink is memory-semantic: a GPU reads and writes a peer GPU's HBM directly via native load/store operations over a lossless fabric, so data transfers are hardware memory operations rather than software sends, and reductions can execute inside the switch itself.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: This architectural choice (in-network compute plus memory-semantic transfers) is what keeps MoE all-to-all and tensor-parallel all-reduce traffic from adding latency, directly enabling the near-linear scaling results later in the piece.
insights.quote: The transfer is a hardware memory operation rather than a software send, so nothing in the data path adds latency, and reductions can run inside the switch as the data flows through.
insights.locator: The width is only half of it; the path is the rest

insights.claim: Beyond the rack, the platform scales out using NVIDIA ConnectX-8 SuperNICs at 800 Gbps per GPU with Quantum-X800 InfiniBand or Spectrum-X Ethernet, designed to keep gradient traffic hidden behind compute across multiple linked racks.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Shows NVIDIA offers a complete two-tier networking stack (NVLink intra-rack + ConnectX-8/InfiniBand/Ethernet inter-rack), a full-stack alternative to buyers mixing third-party networking with NVIDIA GPUs.
insights.locator: Beyond the rack, the platform scales out

insights.claim: NVIDIA BlueField DPUs provide an isolated infrastructure-processing domain for virtual networking, storage access, security, telemetry, and lifecycle management in production AI factories, reducing host CPU overhead for large-scale training jobs.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Offloading infrastructure services to DPUs frees host CPU cycles for training work, a small but compounding efficiency lever at fleet scale.
insights.locator: Co-design extends beyond the training fabric

insights.claim: Scaling DeepSeek-V3 671B pre-training from 256 to 1,024 GPUs, Megatron Core retains 98.5% of its per-GPU performance, while TorchTitan and JAX each retain 97%, meaning nearly all added GPU infrastructure converts directly into added system-level throughput.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Near-linear (97-98.5%) scaling at 4x GPU count is a strong efficiency claim; if it holds in independent benchmarks it undercuts arguments that MoE communication overhead caps large-cluster ROI.
insights.locator: Scaling DeepSeek-V3 671B pre-training from 256 to 1,024 GPUs

insights.claim: NVIDIA engineers contribute directly to open frameworks (TorchTitan with the PyTorch community, JAX with the JAX community) to make them run at full speed specifically on NVIDIA GPUs, rather than relying solely on its proprietary Megatron Core stack.
insights.speaker: Kirthi Devleker
insights.so_what: Embedding hardware-specific optimizations into industry-standard open-source training frameworks deepens developer lock-in to NVIDIA hardware even for teams who avoid NVIDIA's own software stack.
insights.quote: NVIDIA engineers contribute directly to the open frameworks the AI community relies on, adding optimizations that make them faster on NVIDIA GPUs.
insights.locator: Leading pre-training frameworks accelerated by NVIDIA
